name: Barrus Mine pegmatite outcrop
short_description: Explore a classic New England pegmatite outcrop near Goshen where you can find beryl, tourmaline, and feldspar crystals. This accessible site offers a glimpse into the mineral-rich geology of the B...
description:
The Barrus Mine pegmatite outcrop is a productive rockhounding location in the heart of Hampshire County's mineral-rich landscape. This exposure showcases the large-crystal formations typical of New England pegmatites, where you can search for specimens of beryl, tourmaline, feldspar, and quartz. The site sits on gentle slopes at 1,386 feet elevation, making it accessible to most rockhounders without requiring technical climbing or extreme hiking.

The geology here reflects the late-stage crystallization of granitic pegmatite bodies that intruded during the Acadian Orogeny, a major mountain-building event that shaped New England roughly 380 million years ago. These pegmatites formed as the final, volatile-rich melts of large granite plutons cooled slowly underground, allowing mineral crystals to grow to impressive sizes. The Barrus outcrop exposes these coarse crystals in place, making it an excellent location to observe how pegmatites develop and to collect small to medium specimens.

Expect to find well-formed crystals of muscovite mica in sheets, tourmaline in black to dark green prismatic forms, and colorless to pale blue beryl. Feldspar dominates the matrix, appearing as large orthoclase or microcline crystals. The site requires careful examination of the outcrop face and surrounding talus to locate choice specimens. Bring a hand lens to identify minerals and a small pry bar to carefully work specimens free from the rock.

This location is best visited during the warmer months when the ground is dry and accessible. The gentle slope and moderate elevation make it suitable for families and beginning rockhounders, though proper footwear and basic geology knowledge will enhance your experience.
history: The Barrus Mine pegmatite formed during the Acadian Orogeny, approximately 380 million years ago, when continental collision and crustal thickening generated the granitic plutons that host these mineral-rich pegmatite bodies throughout central Massachusetts. The pegmatite crystallized as a late-stage magmatic phase, with slowly cooling volatile-rich melts allowing rare minerals and large crystal specimens to develop, making this outcrop part of New England's classic pegmatite belt.
tips: Search the exposed outcrop face and the talus slope immediately below it for weathered specimens that have loosened from the host rock; many of the best finds emerge from the debris pile. Use a 10x hand lens to identify tourmaline's striations and beryl's hexagonal crystal form before attempting extraction. Work carefully with a small pry bar or chisel to avoid fracturing crystals; tap gently along natural fractures rather than forcing specimens from the matrix. Look for the distinctive blue-green color of beryl in the quartz-rich zones and the black prismatic tourmaline crystals that often cluster at the pegmatite margins.
terrain_notes: Gentle slope terrain at 1,386 feet elevation with mixed deciduous forest and exposed pegmatite outcrop. Loose rock and talus are common around the outcrop face. Walking distance from parking is short (under 0.5 miles). Footing is uneven across the rocky slope, requiring sturdy footwear but not technical climbing ability.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through early fall provides dry ground, accessible trails, and comfortable temperatures. Avoid winter and early spring when wet conditions make the slope slippery.
written_directions: From Goshen center, take MA 112 (Cape Street) northwest for approximately 1.8 miles. Watch for rockhounding access points and pull-offs near the Berkshire Trail West junction. The Barrus Mine outcrop is situated on or near private land in this area; confirm current access status locally before visiting. GPS coordinates 42.472717, -72.838404 will guide you to the approximate location.
road_conditions: MA 112 is a well-maintained secondary highway suitable for all passenger vehicles. Local pull-offs and access roads may be unpaved.
parking_notes: Limited pull-off parking on or near MA 112; confirm access and parking arrangements with local authorities or property owners before visiting.
collection_rules: This site is on private land. Rockhounders must obtain explicit permission from the landowner before collecting. Collection is limited to personal use only; no commercial operations. Remove only what you intend to keep, and avoid damaging the outcrop or leaving excavations.
rules:
Private land - permission required from landowner
Personal use only
No commercial collection
No motorized equipment
Leave no damage to outcrop
nearest_services: Goshen center is approximately 2 miles south; Northampton (larger services) is roughly 12 miles south via MA 112 and MA 9.
accessibility_notes: Relatively gentle terrain on a moderate slope with short walking distance. Uneven, rocky footing requires sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access to the outcrop itself. Children and older rockhounders should move carefully on loose material.

faq.question: What gems and minerals can I find at Barrus Mine pegmatite outcrop near Goshen?
faq.answer: The Barrus Mine pegmatite outcrop is known for beryl (including light blue varieties), black tourmaline, feldspar, quartz, and muscovite mica. You may also find garnets and other pegmatite minerals. Specimens range from small crystals to larger, well-formed pieces suitable for collectors.

faq.question: Is Barrus Mine a free public rockhounding site, or do I need permission?
faq.answer: Barrus Mine pegmatite is located on private land, so you must obtain explicit permission from the landowner before collecting. Contact local Goshen authorities or property owners to confirm current access and collecting rights. There is no fee once you have permission.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Barrus Mine in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: Visit between May and October when the ground is dry and weather is mild. Late spring through early fall provides comfortable temperatures and good footing on the rocky slope. Winter and early spring conditions are wet and slippery.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Barrus Mine pegmatite outcrop for beginners?
faq.answer: Barrus Mine is very beginner-friendly. The site features gentle slopes, short walking distances, and exposed mineral specimens that are easy to identify. Kids as young as 8 can participate with proper supervision. All you need is a hand lens and small pry bar.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring to collect at Barrus Mine pegmatite?
faq.answer: Bring a 10x hand lens to identify minerals, a small pry bar or chisel for careful extraction, sturdy hiking boots for rocky footing, a backpack, water, and a collecting bag. A field guide to New England minerals and protective gloves are also helpful.

faq.question: How far is Barrus Mine pegmatite outcrop from Goshen and major Massachusetts towns?
faq.answer: The Barrus Mine pegmatite is approximately 2 miles northwest of Goshen center via MA 112. Northampton, the nearest larger town with shops and services, is about 12 miles south. Access is straightforward on well-maintained secondary roads.

meta_description: Find beryl, tourmaline, and feldspar at Barrus Mine pegmatite in Goshen, MA. Private land site with gentle terrain perfect for rockhounding beginners.
